response: create_tasks with: task_name='Call the dentist', defer_date='2026-03-16', due_date='2026-03-20'. Defer date hides the task until Monday March 16. Due date is the appointment on Friday March 20.

Step 1: create_projects(name='Kitchen Renovation', project_type='sequential'). Step 2: create_tasks with 5 items in order, each with project_id from step 1: [{task_name: 'Get quotes'}, {task_name: 'Pick contractor'}, {task_name: 'Sign contract'}, {task_name: 'Demolition'}, {task_name: 'Installation'}]. In a sequential project, order determines dependencies — only the first incomplete task is available.
get_tasks(available_only=True). This filters to only actionable tasks, accounting for deferred dates, sequential project blocking, and container status.

update_tasks([{id: 'task-001', flagged: true}, {id: 'task-002', flagged: true}, {id: 'task-003', flagged: true}]). Flagging marks them for today's focus. The Flagged perspective shows all flagged tasks.

update_tasks([{id: 'task-123', task_name: 'Buy groceries'}, {id: 'task-456', flagged: true}, {id: 'task-789', flagged: true}]). A single batch call handles mixed field updates — each item specifies only the fields to change.
update_tasks([{id: 'task-100', project_id: 'proj-200'}]). Setting project_id moves the task from inbox to the specified project.
update_projects([{id: 'proj-888', status: 'dropped'}]). Dropping keeps the project in records but marks it abandoned. Do NOT use delete_projects, which permanently removes it.
reorder_task(task_id='task-B', before_task_id='task-A'). This places task-B before task-A. In a sequential project, order defines dependencies, so task-B will now be available first.

Step 1: create_tasks([{task_name: 'Review PR', tags: ['Computer', 'Work']}]). Tags are set at creation time via the tags parameter. Step 2: update_tasks([{id: 'task-555', add_tags: ['Urgent']}]). Use add_tags to append without replacing existing tags.

update_tasks([{id: 'task-300', due_date: ''}]). Setting due_date to empty string clears the date. Note: if the task's project has a due date, the task will still show an inherited due date in get_tasks results, but the task's own date is cleared.

create_tasks([{task_name: 'Buy nails', parent_task_id: 'task-400'}]). Use parent_task_id to create a subtask. Note: project_id and parent_task_id are mutually exclusive — the subtask inherits the project from its parent. Do NOT pass both.
get_tasks(tag_filter=['Errands', 'Weekend']). The default tag_filter_mode is 'and', so this returns tasks that have BOTH tags.

Multiple calls to build a picture of actionable work: 1) get_tasks(flagged_only=True) — already-flagged priorities. 2) get_tasks(overdue=True) — overdue tasks needing attention. 3) get_tasks(available_only=True, inbox_only=True) — unprocessed inbox items. 4) get_tasks(next_only=True) — next available actions in sequential projects. Then synthesize a focused plan from the results.

Step 1: create_projects([{name: 'Website Redesign', folder_path: 'Work', project_type: 'sequential', review_interval_weeks: 2}]). Step 2: Using the returned project_id, create_tasks([{task_name: 'Wireframes', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}, {task_name: 'Design mockups', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}, {task_name: 'Frontend build', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}, {task_name: 'Backend API', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}, {task_name: 'QA testing', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}, {task_name: 'Launch', project_id: '<id>', tags: ['Web Team']}]). Order of creation in a sequential project determines dependency order. The 'Web Team' tag must already exist.

get_projects(). Review the returned nextReviewDate field for each project. Projects where nextReviewDate is in the past are overdue for review. There is no direct 'review_overdue' filter, so check dates client-side.
update_projects([{id: 'proj-777', status: 'done'}]). This marks the project as completed. Do NOT use 'dropped' — that means abandoned. 'done' means successfully completed.

set_focus does NOT support tasks — it only works with projects and folders. You cannot focus on a single task. Alternative approaches: 1) Flag the task: update_tasks([{id: 'task-999', flagged: true}]) and use the Flagged perspective. 2) Focus on the task's parent project if you want to narrow scope.

Step 1: get_tasks(inbox_only=True) to retrieve all inbox tasks. Step 2: update_tasks with completed=True for each returned task ID: update_tasks([{id: '<id1>', completed: true}, {id: '<id2>', completed: true}, ...]). Note: if any are recurring, completing them will create the next occurrence rather than permanently closing them.

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. Nothing is wrong. Task-700 is an action group (a task with subtasks). The server instructions state: 'Action Groups: Task with subtasks. Parent shows blocked: true while subtasks are active — this is normal, not an error. Do not try to unblock it.' The parent is blocked because it has 3 active subtasks in sequential order. Complete the subtasks and the parent will resolve.

No tool call needed — this is a conceptual question. Yes, that behavior is correct. The 'next' field means 'first available action in a sequential project/action group.' In a parallel project, all incomplete tasks are available simultaneously, so all have next=true. In a sequential project, only the first incomplete task is available (the rest are blocked), so only one has next=true.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation question. CORRECTION: The server instructions say 'Date fields are effective (include inherited from project).' So get_tasks should return the inherited due date from the project. If tasks show empty dueDate despite the project having one, this may be a display inconsistency worth investigating. However, per the tool description, effective dates ARE returned, meaning tasks should show April 15 as their dueDate. You cannot clear an inherited date at the task level — it comes from the project.

No tool call needed — explanation. They may not be the same type. Check the projectType field from get_projects. 'parallel' means tasks can be done in any order but the project has a completion goal. 'single_actions' means a grab-bag list with no completion goal that cannot auto-complete. The deprecated 'sequential' field being false doesn't distinguish between parallel and single_actions — you must check projectType. 'Home Repairs' is likely parallel; 'Errands' is likely single_actions.

No tool call needed — explanation. Using update_tasks([{id: '<task_id>', completed: true}]) on a recurring task WILL create the next occurrence. The server instructions explicitly state: 'completed=True on recurring task creates next occurrence.' It uses the 'mark complete' command internally, which spawns the next recurrence.

update_tasks([{id: '<task_id>', planned_date: '2026-03-18', due_date: '2026-03-20'}]) — or if creating: create_tasks([{task_name: 'Write blog post', planned_date: '2026-03-18', due_date: '2026-03-20'}]). Use planned_date (not defer_date) because planned_date is a scheduling signal only — it doesn't hide the task or affect availability. Defer_date would hide it until that date, preventing earlier work.

create_tasks([{task_name: 'Prepare presentation', defer_date: '2026-03-16', planned_date: '2026-03-18', due_date: '2026-03-20'}]). Three distinct dates: defer_date hides the task until Monday (can't start without materials), planned_date schedules it for Wednesday (when you plan to work on it), due_date is the Friday deadline.
update_tasks([{id: 'task-600', planned_date: ''}]). Empty string clears the planned date.

No tool call needed. Use the repeatSummary field: 'Every week on Mon, Wed, Fri'. The server instructions say: 'always use repeatSummary for display, don't parse RRULE.' The repetitionMethod 'fixed' means it recurs on a fixed schedule regardless of completion date.
update_tasks([{id: 'task-810', recurrence: 'FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=2'}]). Change the RRULE interval from 1 to 2 to make it biweekly.

No tool call needed — explanation. With repetitionMethod 'due_after_completion', the next due date is calculated relative to when you completed the task, not from the original schedule. So the next occurrence will be due one week from today (the completion date), not next Monday.
update_tasks([{id: 'task-900', recurrence: ''}]). Setting recurrence to empty string clears the recurrence rule, making it a one-time task.

update_tasks([{id: 'task-820', recurrence: 'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method: 'due_after_completion'}]). 'due_after_completion' means the next due date is calculated from the actual completion date rather than a fixed calendar schedule.
update_tasks([{id: 'task-830', recurrence: 'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method: 'fixed'}]). 'fixed' keeps it on the same daily schedule regardless of when you complete each occurrence.

update_tags([{id: 'tag-050', status: 'dropped'}]). WARNING: The user wants tasks with this tag to remain available and actionable. However, the 'on_hold' status would make tasks with this tag unavailable (excluded from Available perspective). 'dropped' hides the tag from the picker. But we should check: the user says they want tasks to remain actionable. The server instructions say 'Tags can be Active or On Hold — tasks with On Hold tags are excluded from Available perspective.' Dropped is not described regarding task availability. Dropping the tag should hide it while keeping tasks actionable. Use status 'dropped'.

update_tags([{id: 'tag-052', status: 'active'}]). Setting 'Waiting' to active means tasks with this tag will be available again (on_hold excludes tasks from Available perspective). No change needed for 'Archive' — it's already dropped and should stay as-is.

create_projects([{name: 'Someday/Maybe', project_type: 'single_actions'}]). The 'single_actions' project type has no completion goal and cannot auto-complete when tasks are checked off — exactly what's described.
update_folders([{id: 'folder-999', status: 'dropped'}]). Setting status to 'dropped' hides the folder from view without deleting it. This archives it while preserving all projects and tasks inside.
